data.title: Sensex Ends on Higher Note
data.description:
The BSE Sensex finished about 1.7% higher at 75,157.3 on Friday, reversing yesterday's decline, after US President Donald Trump declared a 90-day pause on tariff hikes for most trading partners, including India.

Meanwhile, global sentiment remains fragile amid rising US-China trade tensions and due to frequent policy flip-flops in the US trade policy.

On the domestic front, India's inflation is projected to stand at 3.6%, the lowest level since last July, raising hopes that the RBI may introduce further interest rate cuts.

Indian markets will be closed on Monday for the Ambedkar Jayanti holiday.

data.title: Ukraine set to extend martial law again, pushing back prospect of elections
data.description:
Key points:
  • Ukraine highly likely to extend martial law again
  • Elections impossible while war with Russia rages, speaker says
  • Stefanchuk says Ukraine, unlike Russia, is committed to democracy
  • Early preparations under way for voting in unprecedented post-war context

Ukrainian lawmakers are almost certain to extend martial law again before it expires on May 9, the parliamentary speaker has said, determined to uphold democracy even as the United States and Russia pressure Kyiv to hold a new vote.

Speaking at the heavily guarded parliament, Ruslan Stefanchuk underlined the practical and legal implausibility of holding free and fair elections in a country that is part-occupied and still under constant attack, three years after Russia's full-scale invasion.

Since invading Ukraine in strength in February 2022, Russia has sought to paint the government and particularly President Volodymyr Zelenskiy, whose five-year term ended last year, as illegitimate.

But Stefanchuk stressed Ukraine's commitment to democratic elections, contrasting it with Russia, which has eliminated political opposition at home and been forced to deny allegations of covert campaigns to influence a host of elections abroad.

"It is a priority for me because Ukraine has always been - historically, and it is now, and will remain - a democratic Ukraine," he told Reuters in an interview on Thursday.

"This is what distinguishes us from the Russian Federation. On this issue, we are on two banks of the civilisation abyss."

Preparations for future elections have begun, he said, but are in the very early stages.

Parliamentary and presidential elections were last held in Ukraine in 2019. Parliamentary approval is required every 90 days to extend martial law, which allows troops to be mobilised and the electoral cycle to be suspended.

NO ELECTIONS WHILE SOLDIERS FIGHT, CIVILIANS SEEK SHELTER

Stefanchuk, who would take over were Zelenskiy to die or be incapacitated, said parliament was almost certain to renew its approval because "the war is not over".

That means about 800,000 potential voters are in uniform: fighting or training.

Nearly five million of Ukraine's pre-war population of about 44 million are registered as internally displaced, while more than four million Ukrainians are registered as living in European Union countries alone, to say nothing of those displaced but unregistered.

Around a fifth of Ukraine, in the south and east, is occupied by Russia.

Although Donald Trump began his second term as U.S. president putting continued military support for Kyiv in question amid overtures to Russia, and demanding a swift end to the war, negotiations are stalled.

Meanwhile, fighting still rages on a front line 1,000 km (600 miles) long, and the towns and cities in the rest of the country live in fear of Russian bombardment.

Even when the conflict ends, recovering enough to hold a proper election will be an enormous challenge, for which Ukraine will need to create the framework from scratch, including a new law to spell out the timing, rules and procedures.

Stefanchuk said lawmakers, electoral officials and other experts were working on the issues, but had not yet begun drafting a bill.

No decision has yet been made on the sequencing of the local, parliamentary and presidential ballots that will all have to be held.

"Even from a theoretical point of view, it's unrealistic to hold all elections at the same time," Stefanchuk said.

Before the war, elections cost around 4 billion hryvnias ($100 million) to stage. But in addition to financing campaigns and voting, Kyiv will also need to guarantee security and pre-empt any possible manipulation attempts by its enemy, Moscow.

data.title: Wells Fargo's Q1 Earnings Rise, Revenue Declines
data.description:
Wells Fargo reported Q1 earnings Friday of $1.39 per diluted share, up from $1.20 a year earlier.

Analysts polled by FactSet expected $1.23.

Total revenue for the quarter ended March 31 was $20.15 billion, down from $20.86 billion a year earlier.

Analysts surveyed by FactSet expected $20.72 billion.

data.title: Orkla Wraps Up Sarpsfoss Sale as Part of Norwegian Hydropower Portfolio Sale
data.description:
Orkla said Friday it completed the first of its two-part deal to sell its entire hydropower portfolio in Norway.

Sarpsfoss owns hydro power production in Sarpefossen and Mossefossen and sold to Hafslund and Svartisen Holding on a 90/10 basis. The sale was approved by the Norwegian Ministry of Energy and competition authorities.

The Norwegian industrial investor, which signed two deals worth 6.1 billion Norwegian kroner in January, said the other agreement has received the required regulatory approvals and that it will update the market once it is completed.

data.title: BRIEF-Wells Fargo Q1 EPS USD 1.39 vs. IBES Estimate USD 1.24
data.description:
Apr 11 (Reuters) - Wells Fargo Q1 net income USD 4,894 million vs. IBES estimate USD 3,982 million.

  • Q1 EPS USD 1.39 vs. IBES estimate USD 1.24
  • Q1 net interest income USD 11,495 million
  • Q1 CET1 capital ratio 11.1%
  • Q1 ROE 11.5%
  • Q1 credit loss provision USD 932 million
  • Q1 revenue USD 20,149 million vs. IBES estimate USD 20,748 million

data.title: JPMorgan Chase & Co. Reports First-Quarter 2025 Financial Results
data.description:
JPMorgan Chase & Co., a leading global financial services firm, has released its financial results for the first quarter of 2025. The company reported strong performance across various metrics, reflecting its robust business operations and strategic initiatives.

Financial Highlights

For the first quarter of 2025, JPMorgan Chase reported a net income of $14.6 billion, translating to $5.07 per share. This marks a 9% increase in net income compared to the same period last year. The firm's reported revenue stood at $45.3 billion, with managed revenue at $46.0 billion, reflecting an 8% year-over-year growth. The return on common equity (ROE) was 18%, while the return on tangible common equity (ROTCE) was 21%.

Business and Operational Highlights

JPMorgan Chase's Consumer & Community Banking (CCB) segment reported net revenue of $18.3 billion, a 4% increase year-over-year. The segment saw a 12% rise in Card Services & Auto net revenue, driven by higher net interest income on revolving balances. The Commercial & Investment Bank (CIB) segment achieved a 12% increase in net revenue, totaling $19.7 billion, with Markets & Securities Services revenue up 19%.

Asset & Wealth Management (AWM) reported a 12% increase in net revenue, reaching $5.7 billion, driven by higher management fees and brokerage activity. The segment's assets under management (AUM) grew to $4.1 trillion, up 15% year-over-year.

Strategic Initiatives and Corporate Developments

During the first quarter, JPMorgan Chase repurchased $7 billion of common stock and announced a 12% increase in the common dividend. The firm also reported a $588 million gain related to the First Republic acquisition. Additionally, the company raised approximately $840 billion of credit and capital, including $60 billion for consumers and $10 billion for U.S. small businesses.

Management's Perspective

Jamie Dimon, Chairman and CEO, highlighted the firm's strong underlying business and financial results. He noted the increase in Investment Banking fees and the robust performance in the Markets business. Dimon also emphasized the firm's commitment to maintaining excess capital and ample liquidity, with a CET1 ratio of 15.4% and $1.5 trillion in cash and marketable securities.

Dimon acknowledged the economic turbulence, including geopolitical tensions and trade-related issues, but expressed confidence in the firm's ability to navigate these challenges. He reiterated JPMorgan Chase's dedication to serving its clients and communities across all environments.

Future Outlook

Looking ahead, JPMorgan Chase remains focused on maintaining its strong capital position and liquidity. The firm continues to monitor the economic landscape and is prepared to adapt to various scenarios. The company's strategic initiatives and robust financial performance position it well for future growth and stability.

SEC Filing: JPMORGAN CHASE & CO [ JPM ] - 8-K - Apr. 11, 2025

data.title: Advance Residence Investment Sets Loan Terms and Interest Rate Hedge
data.description:
Advance Residence Investment has finalized terms for its 1.5 billion yen long-term loan, with a 500 million yen fixed-rate portion set at 1.10625% over six years, according to its Thursday bourse filing.

For the remaining 1 billion yen floating-rate portion, the Reit entered an interest rate swap agreement with Sumitomo Mitsui Trust Bank, locking in a fixed 1.20900% payout against 1-month yen TIBOR for 7.5 years.

data.title: Westshore Terminals to Launch Share Buyback
data.description:
Westshore Terminals Investment late on Thursday said has received TSX approval to make a normal course issuer bid to purchase for cancellation up to 807,118 issued and outstanding common shares, or about 1.3% of the company's stock.

The NCIB will run from April 15 through to April 14, 2026, or when the company has acquired all of the common shares sought under the bid.

Under its current NCIB, which is set to expire April 14, Westshore has purchased 714,009 common shares at the weighted average price of $23.15 per common share.

data.title: India's industrial output rises 2.9% y/y in February
data.description:
India's industrial output (INIP=ECI) grew 2.9% year-on-year in February, government data showed on Friday.

Economists polled by Reuters were expecting a growth of 4%. Industrial output grew at a revised 5.2% in January, compared to the initially-released 5% growth.

Manufacturing output advanced 2.9% in February, electricity generation grew 3.6% and mining activity rose 1.6%, data showed.

These sectors had grown by a revised 5.8%, 2.4% and 4.4%, respectively, in the previous month.

In the April-February period, industrial output increased by 4.1%.

data.title: Wells Fargo profit rises on wealth management strength
data.description:
Wells Fargo's profit rose 6% in the first quarter, driven by robust performance in its wealth management business.

Investment advisory fees and brokerage commissions rose 7% to $3.17 billion in the quarter, driven by higher asset-based fees.

Although equity markets declined in the first quarter, the impact will show up more in the second quarter, analysts have said.

The fourth-largest U.S. lender's net income rose to $4.89 billion, or $1.39 per share, it said on Friday. That compares with $4.62 billion, or $1.20 per share, a year earlier.

data.title: Earnings Flash (FAST) Fastenal Co. Posts Q1 Revenue $1.96B, vs. FactSet Est of $1.95B
MonotaRO Reports 17% Sales Growth in February
data.description:
MonotaRO Co reported a 17% year-on-year increase in March sales, reaching 28.2 billion yen, according to a Thursday filing on the Tokyo Stock Exchange.

The industrial supplies retailer's customer acquisition rate increased to 107,700 new accounts in March.

data.title: JPMorgan's profit jumps on dealmaking, trading boost
data.description:
JPMorgan Chase's first-quarter profit rose 9% as it earned more from dealmaking and trading.

Earnings were $14.6 billion, or $5.07 a share, for the three months ended March 31, the biggest U.S. bank said on Friday. That compares with $13.4 billion, or $4.44 a share, a year earlier.

Investment banking fees rose 12% to $2.2 billion. Trading revenue climbed 21%, higher than the earlier expectations of a low double-digits percentage gain.

Business was largely buoyed by optimism in the first quarter that U.S. President Donald Trump would enact pro-growth policies, ease regulations and lower taxes.

However, the uncertainty surrounding the trade policy has upended those hopes and fueled market turbulence. Trump last week unveiled steep reciprocal tariffs on dozens of countries, only to pause many of them on Wednesday.
